content:
Employee Creation

Purpose

This guide explains how to create an Employee record in the system and the minimum information required to make the employee usable for HR operations (leave, payroll, attendance, approvals, and reporting).

Who Can Create Employees

Only users with the correct HR permissions should create Employee records, typically: - HR Manager - HR User (with create/write rights on Employee) - System Manager (for setup and support)

Preconditions

Before creating an employee, confirm the following records already exist: - Company - Department - Designation - Branch (if used) - Reports To manager (if known) - Employment Type / Contract Type (if configured) - User account (if the employee needs system login)

Navigation

  1. Open Home.

  2. Search for Employee.

  3. Click New.

Employee Creation Steps

  1. In the Employee form, complete identity fields:

  • First Name

  • Middle Name (optional)

  • Last Name

  • Gender

  • Date of Birth

  1. Complete employment details:

  • Company

  • Employee Number (if manual numbering is enabled)

  • Date of Joining

  • Department

  • Designation

  • Employment Type

  • Status (set to Active for current staff)

  1. Complete organization and reporting details:

  • Branch (if applicable)

  • Reports To

  • Grade (if applicable)

  1. Complete contact details:

  • Prefered Email / Company Email

  • Personal Email (optional)

  • Cell Number

  1. Complete payroll and HR control fields where applicable:

  • Holiday List

  • Default Shift

  • Leave Approver

  • Expense Approver

  • Attendance Device ID (if biometric integration exists)

  1. If the employee requires portal/system access:

  • Link a User ID

  • Ensure role assignment is handled by HR/IT policy

  1. Click Save.

Recommended Post-Creation Actions

After saving the employee record: 1. Create or verify user account and role profile. 2. Create employment contract (if your process requires it). 3. Assign leave policy / leave allocation. 4. Confirm payroll inclusion and salary structure assignment. 5. Confirm reporting line and approvers.

Validation Checklist

Use this checklist before considering setup complete: - Employee status is Active. - Department and designation are correct. - Date of joining is correct. - Employee is linked to the correct company. - Approvers are set (leave/expense where required). - Contact and email details are valid. - User ID is linked (if login is required).

Common Errors and Fixes

  • Missing mandatory fields: Complete highlighted mandatory fields then save again.

  • Invalid company/department/designation: Create the missing master records first.

  • No naming series/employee number issue: Confirm Employee naming settings and series permissions.

  • Approver not selectable: Ensure the approver employee/user is active and has required permissions.

  • User link not allowed: Verify the email/user exists and is not linked to another employee incorrectly.

Data Quality Rules

  • Use official names exactly as in legal/HR documents.

  • Use company email as primary where policy requires.

  • Do not duplicate employee records for the same person.

  • Keep status updated (Active, Left, Suspended) for accurate reporting.

Audit and Control

  • Employee creation should follow HR onboarding approval.

  • Changes to key fields (department, designation, reports to, status) should be traceable.

  • Periodically review active employee list for data accuracy.

Related Documents

  • Employee Onboarding Process

  • Contract Management Guide

  • Leave Allocation Guide

  • Payroll Setup Guide

Revision History

  • 2026-05-06: Initial version created.
name: to91i4vr14
title: Home - Employee Creation

content:
Performance Appraisals

Purpose

This guide explains how HR runs performance appraisals end to end: building a reusable template, creating an appraisal cycle for many staff at once, the supervisor review and automatic grading, and how progress and results appear on the HR Management dashboard.

Key Concepts

  • Performance Appraisal Template – the reusable definition of an appraisal: performance areas & criteria, the rating scale, score bands, and the supervisor-comment questions. One template is marked Active and is used by default.
  • Performance Appraisal Cycle – an HR batch covering one evaluation period. HR selects employees once and submits; the system creates one appraisal per employee.
  • Performance Appraisal – the individual record completed by a supervisor for one employee.

1. Set up a template (once)

Go to Performance Appraisal Template and create a new record. On a new template the following are prefilled with sensible defaults that you can edit:

  • Performance Areas – each area with its criteria (e.g. Job Knowledge and Skills, Quality of Work).
  • Rating Scale – pulled from the Performance Appraisal Rating scale reference (1–5 descriptions).
  • Score Bands – the percentage bands that turn a score into a rating (Exceptional, Strong Performer, … Unacceptable) and the recommended action for each.
  • Supervisor Comments – the open-ended questions the supervisor answers (Highlights, Development Needs, etc.).

Tick Active and save. Only one template can be active at a time; activating a new one deactivates the previous. Update questions here at any time – future appraisals pick up the change.

2. Create an appraisal cycle

From the HR Management dashboard open the Performance Appraisal tab and click + New Appraisal Cycle (or create a Performance Appraisal Cycle directly). Set:

  • Evaluation Type and the From / To dates of the period.
  • Appraisal Template – leave blank to use the active template.
  • Employees – add every staff member who should be appraised this cycle.

3. Submit the cycle

Submitting the cycle creates one Performance Appraisal per employee, seeded from the template, and emails each employee’s supervisor (the employee is CC’d) asking them to complete it. Employees with no supervisor (reports_to) are skipped and flagged on the cycle. You can do this for many employees in a single action.

4. Supervisor completes the appraisal

The supervisor opens the appraisal, rates each performance area, answers the comment questions, and submits it back to HR. Email notifications are sent on every workflow transition (to supervisor, to HR, on approval, rejection, or request for clarification).

5. Automatic grading

When the supervisor submits to HR, the appraisal is graded automatically:

  • Total = sum of the area ratings (max 5 per area).
  • Score (%) = total ÷ (number of areas × 5).
  • Performance Rating = the highest score band the percentage meets, with its Recommended Action.

These fields appear in the Performance Score section of the appraisal. Because the maximum scales with the number of performance areas, grading stays correct even if the template changes.

6. HR review

HR approves, rejects, or requests clarification. A cycle is Completed only when every appraisal in it reaches Approved by HR; otherwise it shows as In Progress.

Dashboard

The Performance Appraisal tab on the HR Management dashboard shows:

  • A toggle to list only incomplete cycles, with per-cycle Total / Completed / Pending counts and status.
  • Charts of graded appraisals: Performance Rating Distribution and Average Score by Department, plus headline metrics.

Roles

  • HR Manager creates templates and cycles, submits cycles, and approves/rejects appraisals.
  • Supervisor completes and submits the appraisals for their direct reports.
meta_description: How HR runs performance appraisals: templates, cycles, supervisor review, grading, and the dashboard.
meta_keywords: performance appraisal, template, cycle, grading, HR
name: perfappraisaldoc1
title: Performance Appraisals

content:
Jira Integration

Purpose

This guide explains how to connect the system to Jira Cloud, sync Jira projects and their issues (tasks), and link those issues to ERPNext Tasks so project work stays in one place.

Key Concepts

  • Jira Settings – a single settings record holding the Jira connection (site URL, account email, API token).

  • Jira Project – a copy of a Jira project pulled into the system. Each one carries a child table of its issues/tasks.

  • Jira Issue – a row under a Jira Project mirroring a Jira issue (key, summary, type, status, assignee, dates, etc.).

  • ERPNext Task – the native task record. Jira issues are linked to Tasks with Task Source = Jira.

1. Connect to Jira (once)

Open Jira Settings and enter:

  • Site URL – e.g. https://yourcompany.atlassian.net (must start with https://).

  • Account Email – the Atlassian account email.

  • API Token – created at id.atlassian.com > Security > API tokens.

Save, then click Test Connection. A green message confirms the credentials.

Note on the API Token: it is a password field and shows masked dots after saving. Leave it untouched on later edits – saving the form will keep the stored token. Only type into it when you genuinely want to replace the token.

2. Pull Jira projects

In Jira Settings use:

  • Pull Projects – imports/updates all Jira projects you can access into Jira Project records.

  • Pull Project by Key – previews a single project (e.g. ER) without saving.

3. Pull issues (tasks) for a project

Open a Jira Project and click Pull Issues from Jira. The system fetches every issue in that project and rebuilds the Tasks / Issues table from scratch, so Jira remains the source of truth and re-syncing never creates duplicates or conflicts.

Each issue row stores: Issue Key, Summary, Type, Status, Priority, Assignee, Reporter, Parent Key, Labels, Resolution, Created, Updated, Due Date, and a direct URL to the issue.

4. Link issues to ERPNext Tasks

To create native Tasks from Jira issues, link the Jira project to an ERPNext Project first:

  1. Open the relevant Project and set the Jira Project field to the matching Jira project (e.g. ER).

  2. On the Jira Project, click Pull Issues from Jira.

For every issue, an ERPNext Task is created or updated under that project, with:

  • Task Source = Jira, and the Jira Issue Key / Jira Issue URL recorded for traceability.

  • Subject from the issue summary, Expected End Date from the due date.

  • Status mapped from Jira (Done/Closed/Resolved → Completed, In Progress → Working, In Review → Pending Review, otherwise Open).

  • Priority mapped from Jira (Highest → Urgent, High → High, Medium → Medium, Low/Lowest → Low).

Tasks are matched on the Jira Issue Key, so repeated pulls update the same Task rather than duplicating it. Issues with no linked ERPNext Project are still stored on the Jira Project, but no Task is created until a Project is linked.

Sync behaviour & conflicts

  • The Jira Project issues table is fully rebuilt on each pull – it always reflects current Jira state.

  • ERPNext Tasks are upserted by Issue Key – safe to re-run anytime.

  • The flow is currently one-way (Jira → ERPNext). Changes made in ERPNext are not pushed back to Jira.

Troubleshooting

  • 410 Gone on pull: Jira removed older search endpoints; the integration uses the current /rest/api/3/search/jql endpoint.

  • Connection fails: re-check the Site URL (https), email, and regenerate the API token.

  • No Tasks created: confirm the ERPNext Project has its Jira Project field set.
meta_description: Connect Jira, sync projects and issues, and link Jira issues to ERPNext Tasks.
meta_keywords: jira, integration, issues, tasks, sync, project
name: jiraintegrationdoc1
title: Home - Jira Integration

content:
Project Meetings

Purpose

This guide explains how to record project meeting minutes in the system using the Project Meeting Minutes document, so that attendance, discussions, action points and next-meeting plans are captured in one place and linked to the project.

Key Concepts

  • Project Meeting Minutes – the main record for a single meeting. It is submittable, so once finalised it can be submitted to lock the content.
  • Attendees – a table listing each person present or absent, with their department/company and attendance status.
  • Agenda – a table of agenda items, each with discussion summaries, action points, RACI and timelines.

1. Create the Minutes

There are two ways to start:

  • Open the Project, click the Create action dropdown and choose Meeting Minutes. The project is filled in automatically.
  • Or go to the Project Meeting Minutes list and click New, then pick the project.

2. Meeting Details

  • Meeting Title – a clear name, e.g. Project Y Demo Meeting.
  • Date – the meeting date (defaults to today).
  • Location – the venue, or Virtual for online meetings.
  • Call Recording – paste the recording link if there is one.

3. Attendees

Add a row per person. Set Status to In Attendance for those present, or Absent with Apology for those who sent apologies. Record their Department/Company.

4. Agenda Details

Add a row per agenda item:

  • Agenda – a short label of the issue discussed.
  • Summaries from Discussion – the important points raised.
  • Action Points – what needs to be done.
  • RACI – who is Responsible, Accountable, Consulted and Informed.
  • Timelines – the due dates or timeframes.

5. Next Meeting

Record the Date of the next meeting and its Objective.

6. Save and Submit

Click Save to keep a draft you can keep editing. When the minutes are final, click Submit to lock them. Submitted minutes can still be amended if a correction is needed.

Where to Find Them

All minutes for a project are listed under the project's Connections tab, in the Reports group as Project Meeting Minutes.
name: projectmeetingsdoc1
title: Project Meetings

content:
Lessons Learned

Purpose

A Lessons Learned Report captures the experience of a project so the team can repeat what worked and avoid what did not. The questions are driven by the active Lessons Learned Template (managed by HR/System Admin), so they stay consistent across all projects.

When to Capture Lessons

  • At project closure, or after a major milestone, phase, or significant incident.
  • When the team wants to capture what worked, what did not, and what to repeat or avoid next time.
  • Before handover, so knowledge stays with the organization and not just individuals.

How to Complete the Report

  1. From the project, click Start Lessons Learned Report – the project title and client are pre-filled for you.
  2. Answer the narrative questions. These come from the active Lessons Learned Template and may change over time.
  3. Fill the Root Cause Analysis table: list each issue, its underlying root cause, and the area affected.
  4. Add Recommendations with a priority (High/Medium/Low) so improvements can be ranked.
  5. Define Next Steps as concrete action items with a responsible person, deadline, and status.
  6. Attach supporting documentation, then route to the supervisor for sign-off and submit.
name: lessonslearneddoc1
title: Lessons Learned

content:
High Level Work Plan

Purpose

The High Level Work Plan (HLWP) captures the major line items and key deliverables of a project on a single timeline. It gives the team and stakeholders a top-down view of what will be delivered, when, by whom, and the current status – without the task-level detail.

Key Concepts

  • Project – each HLWP is linked to one Project. The project's Work Plan tab reads from this link.
  • Project Lead, Start/End Date, Duration – header fields summarising ownership and timeline.
  • Work Plan Items – the table of line items, each with a key deliverable, dates, expected outcome, status, resources and comments.
  • Key Deliverable – a linked Project Key Deliverable; new values are created automatically on import.
  • Entry Type – either System Entry (filled in directly) or Template Import (uploaded from an Excel template).

1. Create a High Level Work Plan

Open New → High Level Work Plan and set the Project, Project Lead and the start/end dates. The naming series is HLW-.YYYY.-.MM.-.

2a. System Entry

Set Entry Type = System Entry and add rows to the High Level Workplan Table. For each item provide:

  • Line Item – the activity or work package name.
  • Key Deliverable – the output it produces.
  • Start Date / End Date – the planned window.
  • Expected Outcome – what success looks like.
  • Status – NOT STARTED, IN PROGRESS, or COMPLETED.
  • Resources and Comments – supporting notes.

2b. Template Import

Set Entry Type = Template Import and attach the completed Excel file under Template Import. On save, the rows are read from the spreadsheet and loaded into the table. The importer scans the first rows to detect the header line (line_item, key_deliverable, start_date, end_date, expected_outcome, status, resources, comments), so the column order is flexible. Download the blank template from the project's Work Plan tab using Download Template.

3. View on the Project Dashboard

Open the project in ICL Project Management and go to the Work Plan tab. The High Level Work Plan shows a summary strip (Lead, dates, duration, item count, % completed), an overall completion bar, and the searchable, filterable items table. Use Open Plan to edit the underlying document.
name: hlworkplandoc1
title: High Level Work Plan

content:
Detailed Work Plan

Purpose

The Detailed Work Plan (DWP) breaks a project down to the activity level. Where the High Level Work Plan shows major line items, the DWP lists the individual activities, their level of effort, and the resources assigned, giving the delivery team an executable plan.

Key Concepts

  • Project – each DWP links to one Project and appears on its Work Plan tab.
  • Client – the linked Customer the work is delivered for.
  • Developer / IntelliSOFT Project Lead – the people responsible for delivery.
  • Project Timeline, Start Date, End Date – the overall schedule.
  • Detailed Workplan Items – the activity table.

1. Create a Detailed Work Plan

Open New → Detailed Work Plan, link the Project and Client, and record the developer, lead and timeline. The naming series is DWP-.YYYY.-.MM.-.

2. Add Activities

In the Detailed workplan table add a row per activity with:

  • Item – the activity reference or grouping.
  • Activities – the work to be done.
  • Expected Outcomes & Deliverables – what the activity produces.
  • Resources – who or what is required.
  • Start Date / End Date – the activity window.
  • Duration LOE – level of effort, in days.
  • Status – the current progress of the activity.

3. Google Drive Link

If the plan is maintained as a spreadsheet, paste its shared link in Google Drive link so the team can reach the source document from the record.

4. View on the Project Dashboard

On the project's Work Plan tab, the Detailed Work Plan appears below the High Level plan. Use Open Plan to edit the activities.
name: dworkplandoc1
title: Detailed Work Plan

content:
Project Module

Purpose

The Project module is the central record for everything an engagement needs – team, timeline, budget, work plans, meetings, status reports and lessons learned. This guide walks through creating a project, adding the people who work on it, and where to manage the project day to day.


Key Concepts

  • Project – the master record. Most other documents (Timesheets, Travel Requests, Work Plans, Tasks, Meetings) link back to it.

  • Project Users – the team members granted access, each with an allocated level of effort (LOE).

  • Project Manager(s) – the employee(s) accountable for the project.

  • ICL Project Management – the dashboard for working with a single project across tabs (Overview, Tasks, Team, Timesheets, Documents, Work Plan).

1. Create a Project

Go to New → Project (or the Projects list → Add Project) and fill the core details:

  • Project Name – a clear, unique title.

  • Status – Open, Completed or Cancelled.

  • Customer – the client the work is delivered for.

  • Department, Company and Priority.

  • Expected Start / End Date – the planned timeline.

Save to create the project.

2. Fill Project Information

Use the Project Information and related sections to record context:

  • Organisation, Role and Preferred Communication Method.

  • Donor and Partner Organisations – funding and collaborating bodies.

  • Project Opportunity Bid – link the originating Opportunity if the project came from a bid.

  • Jira Project – link the matching Jira project so issues can sync into Tasks (see the Jira Integration guide).

3. Add Project Users

Open the Users table on the project and add a row per team member:

  1. Select the User – their full name fills in automatically.

  2. Set Allocated LOEs – the level of effort (in days) committed to this project.

  3. Leave Welcome Email ticked if you want the user notified that they were added.

Project users gain access to the project and appear under the Team tab of the dashboard, with their allocated LOEs and total logged hours.

4. Assign Project Manager(s)

In the Project Manager tab, add the accountable employee(s) to the Project Manager(s) table. Each row links an Employee (with department), and project managers receive the project notifications and approvals.

5. Reporting & Notifications

  • Project Report Frequency – how often status reports are expected (e.g. Weekly, Monthly).

  • Allow Notification – enable to send project alerts to the team and managers.

6. Manage the Project in ICL Project Management

Open the ICL Project Management page and select the project to work in the dashboard. Tabs available:

  • Overview – meta, progress, financial snapshot and assignment.

  • Tasks – project tasks with search, pagination, and Pull from Jira when a Jira project is linked.

  • Team – project users with allocated LOEs and hours.

  • Timesheets – timesheets logged against the project.

  • Documents – File Manager and Google Drive folders.

  • Work Plan – High Level and Detailed Work Plans.

Related Guides

  • High Level Work Plan and Detailed Work Plan – planning the delivery.

  • Project Meetings – recording meeting minutes.

  • Lessons Learned – capturing what worked and what to improve.

  • Jira Integration – syncing Jira issues into project Tasks.
name: projectmoduledoc1
title: Home - Project Module

content:
Timesheet Submission

Purpose

The Timesheet Submission is an employee's monthly record of hours worked across projects. It is the document that moves through the approval workflow – from the employee, through review, to Finance – and on final approval the underlying Timesheet records are confirmed in the system.

Key Concepts

  • Timesheet Submission – one per employee per month (naming series TS-). It is submittable, so it locks once Finance approves.
  • Timesheet Template – either Default (regular staff) or Short Term Consultant. This is derived from the employee's Contract Type and decides which approval path the submission follows.
  • Timesheet Per Project – the table listing hours against each project for the month.
  • Total Working Hours – the sum that must be greater than zero before submission.
  • Finance Breakdown – on Finance review, salary (CTC), salary per hour and total billable hours are computed for costing.

1. Create the Submission

  1. Go to New → Timesheet Submission.
  2. Select the Employee – the name and Timesheet Template are derived automatically.
  3. Set the Month-Year the submission covers.

2. Enter Hours and Generate Timesheets

Add the project lines under Timesheet Per Project and generate the underlying Timesheet entries in the system. The workflow enforces two gates before the submission can leave Draft:

  • Total Working Hours must be greater than 0.
  • Timesheet entries must already be inserted for the submission (a generated Timesheet linked to it).

If either is missing, submission is blocked with a message.

3. The Approval Workflow

The path depends on the employee's Timesheet Template. Rejections and clarification requests always return the document to Draft so the employee can correct and resubmit.

Regular Staff (Default template)

Draft → Submitted to Supervisor → Submitted to Finance → Approved by Finance

Short Term Consultant

Draft → Submitted to Project Manager → Submitted to Supervisor → Submitted to Finance → Approved by Finance

Short Term Consultants have an extra Project Manager review first, because their time is billed to specific projects.

States

StateMeaningDocument Status
DraftBeing prepared / returned for correctionDraft
Submitted to Project ManagerAwaiting PM review (Short Term Consultant only)Draft
Submitted to SupervisorAwaiting supervisor reviewDraft
Submitted to FinanceAwaiting Finance reviewDraft
Approved by FinanceApproved and lockedSubmitted

Transitions

FromActionRoleTo
DraftSubmit To SupervisorEmployeeSubmitted to Supervisor (Default)
DraftSubmit to Project ManagerEmployeeSubmitted to Project Manager (Short Term Consultant)
Submitted to Project ManagerApprove by Projects ManagerProjects ManagerSubmitted to Supervisor
Submitted to Project ManagerReject / Request clarificationProjects ManagerDraft
Submitted to SupervisorApprove by SupervisorSupervisorSubmitted to Finance
Submitted to SupervisorReject / Request clarificationSupervisorDraft
Submitted to FinanceApprove by FinanceFinanceApproved by Finance
Submitted to FinanceReject / Request clarificationFinanceDraft

Reviewing & Requesting Clarification

Reviewers do more than approve. At each review stage – Project Manager, Supervisor and Finance – the reviewer can open and examine the submitted timesheet entries and, instead of approving, choose Request for Clarification. This returns the submission to Draft, records the reviewer's note as a comment on the document, and emails the employee the clarification message so they can correct and resubmit.

4. Finance Approval & Outcome

When Finance approves, the submission reaches Approved by Finance and is submitted (locked). At this point the system finalises the linked Timesheet records and computes the finance breakdown (employee CTC, salary per hour and total billable hours) used for project costing.

From an approved submission, Finance can download the timesheet report (Project Ratios) – an Excel export listing the hours per project and each project's ratio (%) of the total (used for QuickBooks billing), followed by a per-project task matrix of the inserted timesheet entries.

5. Reminders & Notifications

Each transition notifies the next approver. Employees who have not submitted for the period can be chased through the non-submitter reminder. Clarification requests carry the reviewer's note back to the employee.

Related Guides

  • Project Module – projects and project users that timesheets are logged against.
name: timesheetsubmissiondoc1
title: Timesheet Submission

content:
Leave Application

Purpose

The Leave Application is how an employee formally requests time off. Each request runs through an approval workflow – from the employee, to their supervisor, to HR – and once approved the days are deducted from the employee's leave balance.

Key Concepts

  • Leave Application – a single request for a date range and leave type. It is submittable, so it locks once HR approves.
  • Leave Type – the category of leave (e.g. Annual, Sick, Compassionate). Each type has its own rules and balance.
  • Leave Allocation / Balance – the days available to the employee for a leave type in the period. The application checks this before approval.
  • Supervisor and HR Manager – the two approval levels.

1. Apply for Leave

  1. Go to New → Leave Application.
  2. Select your Employee record and the Leave Type.
  3. Set the From Date and To Date. Tick Half Day if it is a partial day, and pick the half-day date.
  4. Confirm the Total Leave Days and that the Leave Balance is sufficient.
  5. Enter a Reason for the request.

2. The Approval Workflow

The employee submits the request, which then moves through two review levels. A rejection at either level stops the request.

Draft → Submitted to Supervisor → Submitted to HR → Approved

States

StateMeaningDocument Status
DraftBeing prepared by the employeeDraft
Submitted to SupervisorAwaiting the supervisor's reviewDraft
Submitted to HRApproved by supervisor, awaiting HRDraft
Rejected By SupervisorDeclined by the supervisorDraft
RejectedDeclined by HRDraft
ApprovedApproved by HR and lockedSubmitted

Transitions

FromActionRoleTo
DraftSubmit To SupervisorEmployeeSubmitted to Supervisor
Submitted to SupervisorApprove by SupervisorSupervisorSubmitted to HR
Submitted to SupervisorReject by SupervisorSupervisorRejected By Supervisor
Submitted to HRApprove by HR ManagerHR ManagerApproved
Submitted to HRReject by HR ManagerHR ManagerRejected

3. Notifications

Each step emails the relevant party: the supervisor when a request is submitted, the employee when their supervisor approves (and it moves to HR), and the employee again on the final HR decision (approved or rejected).

4. After Approval

Once HR approves, the application is submitted (locked) and the leave days are deducted from the employee's balance for that leave type. The approved leave then appears on the employee's leave records and calendar.
name: leaveapplicationdoc1
title: Leave Application

content:
Travel Request

Purpose

The Travel Request is how an employee seeks authorisation and funding for a trip. It captures the itinerary, the purpose, the people travelling and the expected support (advance), then runs through an approval workflow from the employee to the supervisor to Finance.

Key Concepts

  • Travel Type – Domestic or International. The form shows the relevant sections for each (local places & transport matrix for Domestic; place-of-travel per diem for International).
  • Currency – the currency the trip is funded in; the Expected Support (advance requested) and amounts are shown in this currency, with a local-currency equivalent.
  • Activity Participants – the people travelling and their costs (accommodation, meals & incidentals), which roll up into the Expected Support total.
  • Per Diem – standard rates pulled from the selected place of travel.

1. Create a Travel Request

  1. Go to New → Travel Request and select the traveller (Employee).
  2. Choose the Travel Type (Domestic or International) and the Currency.
  3. Add the Itinerary (from, to, mode, departure) and, if needed, overnight-stay and duration-of-stay details.
  4. Fill the Activity Participants with each person's accommodation and meals/incidentals; the Expected Support total is calculated for you.
  5. Record the purpose, anticipated outcome and organiser details, then submit for approval.

2. The Approval Workflow

Draft → Submitted to Supervisor → Submitted to Finance → Approved by Finance

States

StateMeaningDocument Status
DraftBeing prepared by the employeeDraft
Submitted to SupervisorAwaiting supervisor reviewDraft
Submitted to FinanceApproved by supervisor, awaiting FinanceDraft
Rejected By SupervisorDeclined by the supervisorSubmitted
Rejected by FinanceDeclined by FinanceSubmitted
Approved by FinanceApproved and lockedSubmitted

Transitions

FromActionRoleTo
DraftSubmit To SupervisorEmployeeSubmitted to Supervisor
Submitted to SupervisorApprove by SupervisorSupervisorSubmitted to Finance
Submitted to SupervisorReject by SupervisorSupervisorRejected By Supervisor
Submitted to FinanceApprove by FinanceFinanceApproved by Finance
Submitted to FinanceReject by FinanceFinanceRejected by Finance

Each step notifies the next party. Where the traveller is the CEO, the supervisor step is skipped automatically and the request goes straight to Finance.

3. After Approval

Once Finance approves, the advance (Expected Support) is authorised. After the trip, the spending is accounted for in a Travel Request Reconciliation – see that guide.
name: travelrequestdoc1
title: Travel Request

content:
Travel Request Reconciliation

Purpose

The Travel Request Reconciliation accounts for what was actually spent against an approved Travel Request advance. It records the real costs, compares them to the advance, and produces a balance (refund due or top-up). It links back to its Travel Request and runs through a Finance approval workflow.

Key Concepts

  • Travel Request – the approved request being reconciled. The currency, advance and traveller are pulled from it.
  • Total Advance – the amount that was approved on the Travel Request.
  • Actual Allocated Amount – used only when the amount actually disbursed differs from the request. Tick Allocated Amount Differs to reveal and use it; otherwise the Total Advance is used.
  • Activity Participants Reconciliation – the line items of actual spend, each with the amount paid and an FX rate that yields the local-currency amount.
  • Total Spent / Total Balance – computed totals in the Travel Request's currency; the balance is allocated amount minus total spent. Total Local Amount shows the local-currency equivalent.

1. Create the Reconciliation

  1. Go to New → Travel Request Reconciliation and select the Travel Request; the traveller, currency and advance are fetched.
  2. Record the dates (advance taken, reconciliation, trip dates) and project budget code(s).
  3. Add the actual spend rows under Activity Participants Reconciliation (amount paid and FX rate). Totals and balance are calculated automatically.
  4. If the disbursed amount differed from the request, tick Allocated Amount Differs and enter the Actual Allocated Amount.
  5. Attach the Trip Report and submit to Finance.

2. The Approval Workflow

Draft → Submitted to Finance → Approved by Finance

States

StateMeaningDocument Status
DraftBeing preparedDraft
Submitted to FinanceAwaiting Finance reviewDraft
Rejected by FinanceDeclined by FinanceSubmitted
Approved by FinanceApproved and lockedSubmitted

Transitions

FromActionRoleTo
DraftSubmit to FinanceAll (the traveller)Submitted to Finance
Submitted to FinanceApprove by FinanceFinanceApproved by Finance
Submitted to FinanceReject by FinanceFinanceRejected by Finance

3. Link Back to the Travel Request

When a reconciliation is approved (submitted), its Travel Request is marked Reconciled and the reconciliation reference, date, actual allocated amount, total spent and balance are written back onto the Travel Request for reporting. Deleting or cancelling the reconciliation reverts the Travel Request to Pending Reconciliation.
name: travelreconciliationdoc1
title: Travel Request Reconciliation

content:
Project Management Lessons Learned - Workflow & Notifications

Purpose

This guide explains the approval workflow for a Project Management Lessons Learned report, which notifications are sent at each step, who receives them, and what to check if an email is not arriving.

Workflow Overview

A Lessons Learned report moves through the following states before it is finalised:

Draft → Submitted to Supervisor → Approved (or Rejected or Needs Clarification)

If the supervisor requests clarification, the reporter addresses the feedback and resubmits:

Needs Clarification → Resubmit → Submitted to Supervisor

Workflow States

StateMeaning
DraftReport is being written or has been returned with a clarification request.
Submitted to SupervisorReporter has submitted; supervisor must review.
Needs ClarificationSupervisor has requested more information; reporter must address and resubmit.
ApprovedSupervisor has approved the report. No further action required.
RejectedSupervisor has rejected the report. The reporter should contact their supervisor.

Transitions

FromActionWho Can ActTo
DraftSubmit To supervisorAll (reporter)Submitted to Supervisor
Submitted to SupervisorApproveSupervisorApproved
Submitted to SupervisorRejectSupervisorRejected
Submitted to SupervisorNeeds ClarificationSupervisorNeeds Clarification
Needs ClarificationResubmitAll (reporter)Submitted to Supervisor

Email Notifications

The system sends an automatic email on every workflow transition. Notifications are triggered by the on_update hook in the Project Management Lessons Learned controller and use the employee's Company Email (falling back to Personal Email) for delivery.

1. Reporter submits → Supervisor is notified

  • Trigger: State changes from Draft to Submitted to Supervisor.
  • Recipient: The employee's supervisor (the employee linked in the Reports To field of the reporter's Employee record).
  • Subject: Lessons Learned Report <name> submitted for review
  • Content: Supervisor's name, reporter's name, report ID, project name, and a direct link to open the record.

2. Reporter resubmits → Supervisor is notified

  • Trigger: State changes from Needs Clarification to Submitted to Supervisor.
  • Recipient: Same supervisor as above.
  • Subject: Lessons Learned Report <name> resubmitted for review
  • Content: Same as the initial submission email but states the report has been resubmitted.

3. Supervisor approves → Reporter is notified

  • Trigger: State changes to Approved.
  • Recipient: The reporter (employee on the report).
  • Subject: Lessons Learned Report <name> - Approved
  • Content: Congratulatory message with report ID, project name, and a link to view the approved record.

4. Supervisor rejects → Reporter is notified

  • Trigger: State changes to Rejected.
  • Recipient: The reporter.
  • Subject: Lessons Learned Report <name> - Rejected
  • Content: Informs the reporter the report was rejected and asks them to contact their supervisor for guidance.

5. Supervisor requests clarification → Reporter is notified

  • Trigger: State changes to Needs Clarification.
  • Recipient: The reporter.
  • Subject: Lessons Learned Report <name> - Needs Clarification
  • Content: Informs the reporter that the report requires clarification and asks them to address the feedback and resubmit.

Prerequisites for Notifications to Work

Both the reporter and the supervisor must have a valid email address in their Employee record before notifications can be sent.

CheckWhere to set it
Reporter has Company Email or Personal EmailEmployee record → Contact section
Reporter's Employee record has a Reports To value (the supervisor)Employee record → Reports To field
Supervisor has Company Email or Personal EmailSupervisor's Employee record → Contact section

If any of the above is missing, the relevant notification is silently skipped - the workflow transition still succeeds and no error is shown.

Troubleshooting

  • Supervisor did not receive the submission email: Check that the reporter's Employee record has a Reports To value and that the supervisor's Employee record has a valid email. Also check the system's Email Queue (Email Queue list in Frappe) for delivery errors.
  • Reporter did not receive the approval/rejection email: Check the reporter's Employee record for a company or personal email address.
  • Email Queue has errors: Verify the outbound email settings in Email Account (SMTP configuration).
  • Workflow action is not available: Confirm that the user performing the action has the correct role (Supervisor for approve/reject/clarification actions).

Related Guides

  • Lessons Learned – how to fill in and submit a Lessons Learned report.
  • Project Module – managing projects, team members, and reports.
meta_description: Workflow states, transitions, and automatic email notifications for the Project Management Lessons Learned approval process.
meta_keywords: lessons learned, workflow, notifications, email, supervisor, approval
name: pmllworkflowdoc1
title: Lessons Learned - Workflow and Notifications

content:
Employee KPI Creation

Purpose

This guide explains how to create an Employee KPI record in the system. KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) define WHAT an employee is expected to deliver during a contract or review period. They form the basis for monthly reflections and quarterly appraisals.

Who Can Create Employee KPIs

  • HR Manager
  • Supervisor (for their direct reports)
  • System Manager (for setup and support)

Preconditions

Before creating an Employee KPI, confirm the following exist:

  • An active Employee record in the system
  • The employee's contract or Job Description is available for reference
  • KPI Categories are configured (if used)
  • The review period / contract period is known

Navigation

  1. Open the ICL Staff workspace.
  2. Under Performance & Development, click Employee KPI.
  3. Click New.

Section A - Employee Information

Complete the header fields that identify the employee and the review period:

  • Employee - select the employee from the list
  • Employee Name - auto-filled from the employee record
  • Job Title - the employee's current job title
  • Department - the employee's department
  • Supervisor - the name of the direct supervisor
  • Review Period - the date marking the start of the review/contract period
  • Contract Type - select the applicable contract type (e.g. Full-time, Consultancy)

Section B - KPI Details Table

Add one row per KPI. Each row captures:

  • KPI# - sequential number for reference (e.g. 1, 2, 3)
  • Strategic Objective - the organisational or departmental goal this KPI supports
  • KPI Category - the category this KPI falls under
  • KPI Description - a clear statement of what the employee must deliver
  • Measure - how performance will be assessed (e.g. % completion, number of reports, rating)
  • Target - the specific result that constitutes success
  • Weight (%) - the relative importance of this KPI; all weights must total 100%
  • Data Source - where evidence of performance will come from (e.g. system report, client feedback)

How to Draft KPIs from a Contract or Job Description

  1. Read the employee's contract and Job Description. Highlight key responsibilities and expected outputs.
  2. Group similar responsibilities into 4–8 main result areas (e.g. project delivery, documentation, client communication, reporting).
  3. For each result area, write one clear KPI that describes the expected result, not just the activity.
  4. For each KPI, define the Measure, Target, and Weight (%).
  5. Ensure all weights add up to 100%. Assign higher weight to more critical areas.

KPI Quality Checklist (SMART)

Each KPI must be:

  • Specific - clearly states what should be achieved
  • Measurable - includes a number, percentage, rating, or deadline that can be verified
  • Achievable - realistic given the time frame and available resources
  • Relevant - linked to the role, department, and organisational goals
  • Time-bound - has a clear period (monthly, quarterly, annual, or contract period)

Saving the Record

  1. Review all rows in the KPI table and confirm weights total 100%.
  2. Click Save.
  3. Share the saved KPI record with the employee for acknowledgement before the review period begins.

Link to Performance Reviews

  • The KPI record is the reference document for the employee's Monthly Reflection.
  • At the end of each quarter, the supervisor uses the same KPIs when completing the Appraisal.
  • Do not change KPIs mid-period unless there is a formal role change or contract amendment.

Common Errors and Fixes

  • Employee not selectable - confirm the Employee record exists and is Active.
  • Weights do not total 100% - adjust individual KPI weights before saving.
  • KPI Category not found - create the required category in the master list first.
  • Blank Measure or Target - every KPI must have both a Measure and a Target to be useful for appraisal.

Related Documents

  • Performance Appraisals
  • Employee Creation

Revision History

  • 2026-07-01: Initial version created.
meta_description: Step-by-step guide for creating Employee KPI records in the ICL Corporate Services system.
name: employeekpidoc1
title: Employee KPI Creation
